message.content: ive been exploring a cost explosion in implement engines. see analysis here: `/workspace/nexus-mk2/.scratch/implement-cost-analysis-apr25.md`. Can you capture the initial question, various hypotheses and findings as a tree of clicks so we have it in our thinking? Then, see if there are any experiments related to the findings here that we should update

content.content:
1	# Implement Engine Cost Analysis — April 25, 2026
2	
3	Investigation into why implement engine session costs rose ~12× from early April to late April.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Per-Day Cost and Token Breakdown (completed implement rigs)
8	
9	| Day | n | avg $ | output ktok | cache_read ktok | cache_write ktok |
10	|---|---|---|---|---|---|
11	| Apr 4  |  8 | $0.65 |   8.4k |   668k |  32k |
12	| Apr 6  | 14 | $1.24 |  18.7k |  1813k |  54k |
13	| Apr 7  | 19 | $3.25 |  41.6k |  3651k |  97k |
14	| Apr 8  |  7 | $3.24 |  40.5k |  5408k |  97k |
15	| Apr 9  | 12 | $2.21 |  21.0k |  2219k |  67k |
16	| Apr 10 | 20 | $1.79 |  15.5k |  1770k |  53k |
17	| Apr 13 |  2 | $2.14 |  14.4k |  2235k |  88k |
18	| Apr 14 |  3 | $3.06 |  19.9k |  4005k |  71k |
19	| Apr 15 |  5 | $4.17 |  23.1k |  4716k |  88k |
20	| **Apr 16** | **10** | **$4.80** | **33.6k** | **5636k** | **140k** | ← breakpoint |
21	| **Apr 17** |  **7** | **$8.08** | **56.8k** | **9879k** | **239k** | ← step-change |
22	| Apr 18 |  2 | $10.38 |  72.1k | 13008k | 269k |
23	| Apr 19 |  3 |  $4.98 |  31.8k |  6446k | 140k |
24	| Apr 21 |  9 |  $6.93 |  47.0k |  8440k | 219k |
25	| Apr 22 |  1 | $24.27 | 115.2k | 38163k | 369k |
26	| Apr 23 | 21 |  $5.62 |  33.1k |  7808k | 142k |
27	| Apr 24 | 30 |  $7.99 |  35.1k | 12201k | 147k |
28	| Apr 25 |  4 |  $8.56 |  50.4k | 11934k | 212k |
29	
30	**Summary:** avg cost $0.65 → $8.56 (13×). cache_read tokens 668k → 11,934k (18×). Output tokens 8k → 50k (6×).
31	
32	---
33	
34	## Breakpoint Identification
35	
36	**Apr 16–17** is the clear step-change: avg cost doubles in one day ($4.80 → $8.08), output tokens 1.7×, cache reads 1.75×. All three token categories move together, consistent with longer sessions rather than a system prompt size change.
37	
38	---
39	
40	## Hypotheses Explored
41	
42	### ❌ System prompt size explosion
43	The Loom assembles: charter + tool instructions + role doc. In vibers:
44	- No `charter.md` exists → zero charter contribution
45	- `roles/artificer.md` = 1,522 bytes
46	- The `artificer` role has `clerk:*` and `tools:*` permissions — modest tool set
47	
48	System prompt is small and didn't change around Apr 16-17. **Not the cause.**
49	
50	### ❌ givensSpec input size growth
51	Checked givensSpec total sizes for implement sessions across the timeline:
52	- Apr 6:  41,447 chars
53	- Apr 10: 21,152 chars
54	- Apr 17: 20,507 chars / 26,489 chars
55	- Apr 23:  5,912 chars
56	
57	No correlation with cost. **Not the cause.**
58	
59	### ❌ Repo size as primary driver
60	Repo grew 4.3× (1.0MB → 4.3MB, 144 → 335 files) over the period. Cost grew 13×. Repo growth is a headwind but doesn't explain the discrete Apr 16-17 step-change. **Partial factor, not primary cause.**
61	
62	### ✅ Session length explosion → cache read amplification
63	The primary mechanism: longer sessions cause Claude's automatic cache checkpoints to be re-read on every subsequent turn. Cache reads are cheap per-token ($0.30/Mtok) but the volume grew 18× — from 668k to 12M tokens avg per session. This is a **session length effect**: each additional turn re-reads all previous cache checkpoints.
64	
65	---
66	
67	## Root Cause: Two Commits on Apr 16–17
68	
69	### Commit 1: `920e65ca` — Apr 16 04:43
70	**"feat(astrolabe,spider): add GSD-style task manifest to planner output"**
71	
72	The sage-writer now appends a `<task-manifest>` XML block (3–8 tasks) to every planning output. Each task has `<verify>` (an executable command) and `<done>` (outcome criterion).
73	
74	The implement engine's `EXECUTION_EPILOGUE` changed from:
75	```
76	\n\nCommit all changes before ending your session.
77	```
78	to structured rules: work task-by-task, run each `<verify>` command after each task, commit after each task.
79	
80	**Behavioural shift:**
81	- Before: read writ → write code → commit → done
82	- After: for each task: write code → run verify → review output → commit → repeat
83	
84	This turned a bounded single-pass session into an iterative multi-task loop.
85	
86	### Commit 2: `260f5cf9` — Apr 17 17:01
87	**"sage-writer: inline click content, do not preserve ids in the brief"**
88	
89	Paired with `da0e460` granting sage roles `ratchet:read`. The sage-writer now resolves every click reference in the brief/decisions (`click-extract`, `click-show`) and inlines the full rationale into the spec, making the planning output and the `writ.body` substantially larger and more detailed.
90	
91	This fed forward into implement sessions (especially via `${yields.plan-finalize.spec}` in the plan-and-ship rig, introduced Apr 18-19), producing longer and richer prompts for the implementer.
92	
93	---
94	
95	## Quality vs Cost Tradeoff
96	
97	The cost increase coincided with **measurably better work**:
98	
99	| Metric | pre-Apr16 | post-Apr16 |
100	|---|---|---|
101	| Rig success rate | 83% | 94% |
102	| Avg git commits/session | 0.8 | 1.7 |
103	| Multi-commit sessions | 4% | ~20% |
104	| Avg turns/session | 77 | ~150 |
105	| Avg verify calls/session | 5.5 | 16 |
106	| Zero-commit sessions | ~20% | ~5% |
107	
108	The success rate improvement (83% → 94%) and the near-elimination of zero-commit sessions suggest the structured task manifest is producing better implementation discipline. The cost is real but not waste.
109	
110	**Verify call distribution:** Average position of verify calls in session timeline was 0.58 pre and 0.58 post — identical. The task manifest didn't move verification to earlier in sessions; it **amplified** behavior already latent. What changed was volume (5.5 → 16 verify calls avg), not placement. The agents were already verifying incrementally.
111	
112	---
113	
114	## Recommendations to Reduce Cost (ranked by impact vs effort)
115	
116	### 1. Verify engine as a separate rig stage ★★★ (high impact, clean architecture)
117	Split the rig: `implement` (write code, commit — no tests) + `verify` engine (fresh session, runs test suite, reports pass/fail). If verify fails, post a narrow patch mandate. Implement sessions get dramatically cheaper — bounded by "write and commit." Verification still happens but in a context-free session where it's cheap. This is architecturally correct: what-to-build vs did-it-work are separate concerns. The `implement-loop` / `piece-session` plumbing from Apr 16 (`8d2f548`) was exploring this direction before it was disabled (`fb64473`).
118	
119	### 2. Narrow the verify commands in the task manifest ★★★ (medium impact, low effort)
120	The manifest currently doesn't constrain scope — `pnpm -w test` runs the entire monorepo suite every task. Requiring the sage-writer to produce `pnpm --filter @shardworks/affected-package test` (package-scoped) instead would cut tool_result size, execution time, and the output token cost of reading test results. Change is in `sage-writer.md` manifest rules.
121	
122	### 3. Checkpoint-and-fresh-session architecture ★★ (high impact, medium complexity)
123	After each task commit, end the session and start a fresh one with a compact handoff: "tasks t1-t3 complete, here's the git log, continue from t4." Each session's cache starts small and doesn't compound. This is implement-loop done right — cost per piece is bounded. With 5-task manifests, this could be 5 × $1 instead of 1 × $8. Complexity: the handoff prompt needs enough context for orientation without re-reading everything.
124	
125	### 4. Defer all verification to seal engine ★★ (medium impact, easy, quality tradeoff)
126	The `seal` engine runs next before merging. Add a test-run as seal's first step. If tests fail, seal fails and posts a narrowly-scoped patch mandate. Implement becomes: write code → commit → done. Cheaper per session. Tradeoff: seal doesn't have the implementation context for intelligent failure diagnosis. Mid-task test failures caught late are harder to fix. Acceptable if seal failures are rare.
127	
128	### 5. Verify-reasoning subagent for failures only ★ (targeted, low effort)
129	Normal `pnpm test` runs inline (cheap). When a verify step *fails*, spawn a subagent: "here's the test failure, here's the diff, diagnose and commit a fix." The diagnostic chain-of-thought stays in the subagent; only the fix summary returns to the parent. Subagents don't help for pure command execution (output lands in parent context anyway) but help when failure-diagnosis reasoning is substantial.
130	
131	### 6. Bound manifest body size ★ (small improvement, very low effort)
132	Require sage-writer to cap total manifest to 6 tasks and `<action>` fields to 150 words each. Smaller initial prompt → smaller turn-1 cache write → cheaper every-turn cache re-read. Add to `sage-writer.md` manifest rules.
133	
134	---
135	
136	## Current Understanding
137	
138	The implement engine is paying for **deliberateness**. The task manifest made sessions longer, more incremental, and better. The cost mechanism is Claude's cache being re-read on every additional turn — not a system prompt explosion, not input size growth, but compound re-reading of a growing conversation history. Every verify cycle (2-3 turns) adds to the stack that every subsequent turn has to re-read.
139	
140	The best intervention is structural: separate implementation (expensive, context-rich) from verification (cheap, context-free) as distinct rig stages. This preserves the quality gains while cutting implement session length roughly in half.
141

# Implement Engine Cost Analysis — April 25, 2026

Investigation into why implement engine session costs rose ~12× from early April to late April.

---

## Per-Day Cost and Token Breakdown (completed implement rigs)

| Day | n | avg $ | output ktok | cache_read ktok | cache_write ktok |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apr 4  |  8 | $0.65 |   8.4k |   668k |  32k |
| Apr 6  | 14 | $1.24 |  18.7k |  1813k |  54k |
| Apr 7  | 19 | $3.25 |  41.6k |  3651k |  97k |
| Apr 8  |  7 | $3.24 |  40.5k |  5408k |  97k |
| Apr 9  | 12 | $2.21 |  21.0k |  2219k |  67k |
| Apr 10 | 20 | $1.79 |  15.5k |  1770k |  53k |
| Apr 13 |  2 | $2.14 |  14.4k |  2235k |  88k |
| Apr 14 |  3 | $3.06 |  19.9k |  4005k |  71k |
| Apr 15 |  5 | $4.17 |  23.1k |  4716k |  88k |
| **Apr 16** | **10** | **$4.80** | **33.6k** | **5636k** | **140k** | ← breakpoint |
| **Apr 17** |  **7** | **$8.08** | **56.8k** | **9879k** | **239k** | ← step-change |
| Apr 18 |  2 | $10.38 |  72.1k | 13008k | 269k |
| Apr 19 |  3 |  $4.98 |  31.8k |  6446k | 140k |
| Apr 21 |  9 |  $6.93 |  47.0k |  8440k | 219k |
| Apr 22 |  1 | $24.27 | 115.2k | 38163k | 369k |
| Apr 23 | 21 |  $5.62 |  33.1k |  7808k | 142k |
| Apr 24 | 30 |  $7.99 |  35.1k | 12201k | 147k |
| Apr 25 |  4 |  $8.56 |  50.4k | 11934k | 212k |

**Summary:** avg cost $0.65 → $8.56 (13×). cache_read tokens 668k → 11,934k (18×). Output tokens 8k → 50k (6×).

---

## Breakpoint Identification

**Apr 16–17** is the clear step-change: avg cost doubles in one day ($4.80 → $8.08), output tokens 1.7×, cache reads 1.75×. All three token categories move together, consistent with longer sessions rather than a system prompt size change.

---

## Hypotheses Explored

### ❌ System prompt size explosion
The Loom assembles: charter + tool instructions + role doc. In vibers:
- No `charter.md` exists → zero charter contribution
- `roles/artificer.md` = 1,522 bytes
- The `artificer` role has `clerk:*` and `tools:*` permissions — modest tool set

System prompt is small and didn't change around Apr 16-17. **Not the cause.**

### ❌ givensSpec input size growth
Checked givensSpec total sizes for implement sessions across the timeline:
- Apr 6:  41,447 chars
- Apr 10: 21,152 chars
- Apr 17: 20,507 chars / 26,489 chars
- Apr 23:  5,912 chars

No correlation with cost. **Not the cause.**

### ❌ Repo size as primary driver
Repo grew 4.3× (1.0MB → 4.3MB, 144 → 335 files) over the period. Cost grew 13×. Repo growth is a headwind but doesn't explain the discrete Apr 16-17 step-change. **Partial factor, not primary cause.**

### ✅ Session length explosion → cache read amplification
The primary mechanism: longer sessions cause Claude's automatic cache checkpoints to be re-read on every subsequent turn. Cache reads are cheap per-token ($0.30/Mtok) but the volume grew 18× — from 668k to 12M tokens avg per session. This is a **session length effect**: each additional turn re-reads all previous cache checkpoints.

---

## Root Cause: Two Commits on Apr 16–17

### Commit 1: `920e65ca` — Apr 16 04:43
**"feat(astrolabe,spider): add GSD-style task manifest to planner output"**

The sage-writer now appends a `<task-manifest>` XML block (3–8 tasks) to every planning output. Each task has `<verify>` (an executable command) and `<done>` (outcome criterion).

The implement engine's `EXECUTION_EPILOGUE` changed from:
```
\n\nCommit all changes before ending your session.
```
to structured rules: work task-by-task, run each `<verify>` command after each task, commit after each task.

**Behavioural shift:**
- Before: read writ → write code → commit → done
- After: for each task: write code → run verify → review output → commit → repeat

This turned a bounded single-pass session into an iterative multi-task loop.

### Commit 2: `260f5cf9` — Apr 17 17:01
**"sage-writer: inline click content, do not preserve ids in the brief"**

Paired with `da0e460` granting sage roles `ratchet:read`. The sage-writer now resolves every click reference in the brief/decisions (`click-extract`, `click-show`) and inlines the full rationale into the spec, making the planning output and the `writ.body` substantially larger and more detailed.

This fed forward into implement sessions (especially via `${yields.plan-finalize.spec}` in the plan-and-ship rig, introduced Apr 18-19), producing longer and richer prompts for the implementer.

---

## Quality vs Cost Tradeoff

The cost increase coincided with **measurably better work**:

| Metric | pre-Apr16 | post-Apr16 |
|---|---|---|
| Rig success rate | 83% | 94% |
| Avg git commits/session | 0.8 | 1.7 |
| Multi-commit sessions | 4% | ~20% |
| Avg turns/session | 77 | ~150 |
| Avg verify calls/session | 5.5 | 16 |
| Zero-commit sessions | ~20% | ~5% |

The success rate improvement (83% → 94%) and the near-elimination of zero-commit sessions suggest the structured task manifest is producing better implementation discipline. The cost is real but not waste.

**Verify call distribution:** Average position of verify calls in session timeline was 0.58 pre and 0.58 post — identical. The task manifest didn't move verification to earlier in sessions; it **amplified** behavior already latent. What changed was volume (5.5 → 16 verify calls avg), not placement. The agents were already verifying incrementally.

---

## Recommendations to Reduce Cost (ranked by impact vs effort)

### 1. Verify engine as a separate rig stage ★★★ (high impact, clean architecture)
Split the rig: `implement` (write code, commit — no tests) + `verify` engine (fresh session, runs test suite, reports pass/fail). If verify fails, post a narrow patch mandate. Implement sessions get dramatically cheaper — bounded by "write and commit." Verification still happens but in a context-free session where it's cheap. This is architecturally correct: what-to-build vs did-it-work are separate concerns. The `implement-loop` / `piece-session` plumbing from Apr 16 (`8d2f548`) was exploring this direction before it was disabled (`fb64473`).

### 2. Narrow the verify commands in the task manifest ★★★ (medium impact, low effort)
The manifest currently doesn't constrain scope — `pnpm -w test` runs the entire monorepo suite every task. Requiring the sage-writer to produce `pnpm --filter @shardworks/affected-package test` (package-scoped) instead would cut tool_result size, execution time, and the output token cost of reading test results. Change is in `sage-writer.md` manifest rules.

### 3. Checkpoint-and-fresh-session architecture ★★ (high impact, medium complexity)
After each task commit, end the session and start a fresh one with a compact handoff: "tasks t1-t3 complete, here's the git log, continue from t4." Each session's cache starts small and doesn't compound. This is implement-loop done right — cost per piece is bounded. With 5-task manifests, this could be 5 × $1 instead of 1 × $8. Complexity: the handoff prompt needs enough context for orientation without re-reading everything.

### 4. Defer all verification to seal engine ★★ (medium impact, easy, quality tradeoff)
The `seal` engine runs next before merging. Add a test-run as seal's first step. If tests fail, seal fails and posts a narrowly-scoped patch mandate. Implement becomes: write code → commit → done. Cheaper per session. Tradeoff: seal doesn't have the implementation context for intelligent failure diagnosis. Mid-task test failures caught late are harder to fix. Acceptable if seal failures are rare.

### 5. Verify-reasoning subagent for failures only ★ (targeted, low effort)
Normal `pnpm test` runs inline (cheap). When a verify step *fails*, spawn a subagent: "here's the test failure, here's the diff, diagnose and commit a fix." The diagnostic chain-of-thought stays in the subagent; only the fix summary returns to the parent. Subagents don't help for pure command execution (output lands in parent context anyway) but help when failure-diagnosis reasoning is substantial.

### 6. Bound manifest body size ★ (small improvement, very low effort)
Require sage-writer to cap total manifest to 6 tasks and `<action>` fields to 150 words each. Smaller initial prompt → smaller turn-1 cache write → cheaper every-turn cache re-read. Add to `sage-writer.md` manifest rules.

---

## Current Understanding

The implement engine is paying for **deliberateness**. The task manifest made sessions longer, more incremental, and better. The cost mechanism is Claude's cache being re-read on every additional turn — not a system prompt explosion, not input size growth, but compound re-reading of a growing conversation history. Every verify cycle (2-3 turns) adds to the stack that every subsequent turn has to re-read.

The best intervention is structural: separate implementation (expensive, context-rich) from verification (cheap, context-free) as distinct rig stages. This preserves the quality gains while cutting implement session length roughly in half.

1	---
2	status: active
3	---
4	
5	# X010 — Staged Sessions
6	
7	## Research Question
8	
9	What is the relationship between session length, cost, and output quality for autonomous commissions? Specifically: do long sessions cost more per unit of productive work? Do they produce worse output? And if either is true, does breaking work into shorter sessions capture the benefits without unacceptable handoff losses?
10	
11	## Background
12	
13	There are two independent reasons to suspect that shorter sessions might be better than longer ones:
14	
15	**Cost.** LLM API pricing is token-based. Each turn in a session sends the full conversation history as input. As a session gets longer, every new turn pays for a larger input context — even with caching, cache-read tokens aren't free. This means the cost of each successive turn grows with the session's accumulated context. A 50-turn session doesn't cost 50× a 1-turn session; it costs significantly more, because turns 40-50 are each paying to re-read the context from turns 1-39.
16	
17	**Quality.** LLM attention degrades over long contexts. Earlier decisions get "forgotten," the agent loses coherence with its own prior work, and output quality may decline — especially in long autonomous sessions with heavy tool use, where each turn adds tool calls and results to the context. Shorter, focused sessions might simply produce tighter work: fewer loose ends, less scope drift, more coherent commits.
18	
19	These are independent claims. Cost might be a problem even if quality is fine. Quality might be a problem even if cost is acceptable. Both might be problems, or neither. The experiment needs to measure them separately.
20	
21	Beyond per-session economics, there are **systemic costs** to long-running commissions. A commission that holds a worktree for 30 minutes while other commissions are running has a longer window for `main` to diverge, increasing the probability of a failed fast-forward merge. Shorter sessions (or smaller commissions) reduce this conflict window. Failed merges are expensive — the commission's entire output is discarded, the tokens are wasted, and the work must be re-done or manually recovered. The expected cost of merge failures should factor into the economic analysis.
22	
23	This connects to X007:H2 (Orientation Cost Dominates), which measures how much of a session is spent orienting. X010 asks the upstream question: is the cost/quality profile of long sessions bad enough that paying the re-orientation tax multiple times is still a net win?
24	
25	### Relationship to X004
26	
27	X004 (Iteration Context) asks a closely related question: when an agent iterates on its own prior work, how much context about prior sessions should it get? Its three variants — no context, summary context, full context (via session resume/fork) — directly parallel the handoff problem in staged sessions. X010's "stage notes" are essentially X004's "summary context" variant.
28	
29	Key differences:
30	
31	- **X004 is about iteration on completed work** (amendments to a delivered commission). X010 is about continuation of in-progress work within a single commission.
32	- **X004 includes a "full context" variant** (resume the prior session with full history). X010's premise is that fresh sessions are better — it doesn't test resumption.
33	- **X004 asks whether context can hurt** (over-anchoring on prior approach). X010 assumes the summary helps and asks whether it's *sufficient*.
34	
35	Findings from either experiment inform the other. If X004 finds that summary context outperforms full context, that validates X010's stage-notes approach. If X010 finds that staged sessions lose too much context, that suggests X004's "full context" variant may be necessary for iteration too. Data collection instruments (orientation cost analysis, quality assessment) are shared.
36	
37	## Hypotheses
38	
39	### H1 — Long Sessions Have Increasing Marginal Cost
40	
41	The cost per productive turn increases as a session gets longer. Specifically: the last 10 turns of a long session (30+ turns) cost meaningfully more than the first 10 turns, even though the productive work per turn is comparable. The dominant factor is accumulated input context — each turn pays to re-read the entire conversation history.
42	
43	This is partly true by construction (input context grows, so input tokens per turn grow), but the question is *how much it matters* in practice. With aggressive caching, the marginal cost increase might be trivial. Or it might be substantial — that's what we need to measure.
44	
45	**Measurement:**
46	- Analyze session transcripts from commissioned sessions (existing or new)
47	- For each turn, extract: input tokens (fresh + cache-read + cache-write), output tokens, estimated cost
48	- Plot cost-per-turn over the session lifetime
49	- Calculate: what percentage of total session cost is "context tax" (input tokens that are re-reads of prior conversation, not new information)?
50	
51	**Thresholds:**
52	- **Confirmed:** The last quarter of turns costs >2× per turn compared to the first quarter, consistently across sessions. Context tax accounts for >30% of total session cost.
53	- **Partially confirmed:** Marginal cost increases but modestly (1.3-2× ratio). Context tax is 15-30%. Optimization is a nice-to-have.
54	- **Refuted:** Marginal cost increase is <1.3× (caching absorbs most of it). Context tax is <15%. Long sessions are cost-efficient enough — no cost-motivated reason to stage.
55	
56	### H2 — Shorter Sessions Produce Better Output
57	
58	Independent of cost, shorter focused sessions produce higher-quality work than long sessions tackling the same total scope. Quality here means: fewer defects, less scope drift, tighter task adherence, more coherent code.
59	
60	The mechanism would be cognitive focus — a fresh context window lets the agent bring full attention to a well-scoped chunk of work, rather than juggling an increasingly large mental model. This is analogous to the human experience of "fresh eyes" after a break.
61	
62	**Measurement:**
63	- Run the same commission both ways: single long session vs. manually staged (2-3 shorter sessions with handoff notes)
64	- Compare output quality:
65	  - Defect count (bugs, broken tests, incomplete implementations)
66	  - Scope drift (changes outside the commission scope)
67	  - Code coherence (does the code contradict itself? do later changes undo or clash with earlier ones?)
68	  - Task adherence (did the output match the spec?)
69	  - Commit quality (message clarity, atomic commits vs. sprawling ones — a proxy for agent coherence)
70	- Also compare within single long sessions: is the quality of work in the first half measurably better than the second half?
71	
72	**Thresholds:**
73	- **Confirmed:** Staged output has fewer defects and less drift than single-session output, consistently. And/or: late-session work within a single session is measurably worse than early-session work.
74	- **Partially confirmed:** Quality difference is visible but small. Some commission types show it more than others.
75	- **Refuted:** No measurable quality difference. Long sessions maintain coherence fine. The "context degradation" concern is overblown for sessions at our current scale.
76	
77	### H3 — Systemic Costs Compound the Case
78	
79	Beyond per-session economics, long-running commissions carry systemic costs that shorter sessions avoid:
80	
81	- **Merge conflict probability:** A commission that runs for 30 minutes has a larger window for concurrent commissions to merge to `main` first, causing a fast-forward failure. The cost of a failed merge is high — all tokens spent on the commission are wasted, and the work must be re-done or manually recovered.
82	- **Time-to-feedback:** A long session delays the moment the patron can review the work. Shorter sessions (especially if each stage merges independently) provide faster feedback loops.
83	- **Blast radius:** If a long session goes off the rails at turn 40, turns 1-39 of useful work may be lost. Staged sessions that commit and (potentially) merge incrementally contain the blast radius.
84	
85	Note: the current staged commissions design (see [`artifacts/staged-commissions-spec.md`](artifacts/staged-commissions-spec.md)) keeps all stages on one branch — the merge only happens at the end. This means staging *does not* reduce merge conflict probability in its current form. A variant design where each stage merges to `main` before the next stage starts *would* reduce conflict probability, but requires each stage to be independently mergeable. This is a design question the experiment should inform.
86	
87	**Note:** The staged commissions implementation spec is still a draft with open design questions — particularly around the stage notes file path, loop guards, crash behavior, prompt structure for continuation sessions, and whether stages should merge independently. This experiment should inform those decisions.
88	
89	**Measurement:**
90	- Track merge outcomes (success/fail) across all commissions, correlated with session duration
91	- For failed merges: what was the wall-clock time of the commission? How far had `main` moved?
92	- Estimate the expected cost of merge failures: `P(failure) × cost_of_session`
93	- Compare: would shorter sessions have avoided the conflict?
94	
95	**Thresholds:**
96	- **Confirmed:** Merge failure rate correlates with session duration. Expected cost of failures is significant relative to session cost.
97	- **Refuted:** Merge failures are rare regardless of session length (because concurrent commissions in the same workshop are rare, or because work doesn't overlap).
98	
99	### H4 — Net Economics Favor Staging (Above a Threshold)
100	
101	Combining H1-H3: for commissions above some size threshold, the total cost of multiple staged sessions (including re-orientation tax per stage) is lower than a single long session, with equal or better quality. Below that threshold, single sessions are fine.
102	
103	This is the synthesis hypothesis — it depends on the findings from H1, H2, and H3. The goal is to find the crossover point, if one exists.
104	
105	**Measurement:**
106	- For each commission pair (single vs. staged), compute:
107	  - Total cost (USD) for each variant
108	  - Cost per useful commit as an efficiency proxy
109	  - Re-orientation cost per stage (via X007:H2 analysis tool)
110	  - Quality-adjusted cost (if staged quality differs, factor that in)
111	  - Expected merge failure cost (from H3 data)
112	- Look for the crossover: at what single-session length does staging become net-positive?
113	
114	**Possible outcomes:**
115	- **Clear crossover exists:** Staging is better above N turns (or $N). Build the mechanism, set guidance for commission sizing.
116	- **Staging always wins:** Multiple short sessions are better at every scale. Make it the default, and teach the sage to decompose work accordingly.
117	- **Staging never wins:** Re-orientation cost and handoff losses eat any savings. Single sessions are fine. Invest in better commission specs instead.
118	- **Depends on commission type:** Feature additions hand off cleanly; deep refactors don't. The mechanism is useful but not universal — needs guidance on when to use it.
119	- **Just write smaller commissions:** The real lesson is that the patron (or sage) should decompose work into smaller independent commissions rather than using staging. Each commission is self-contained, merges independently, and doesn't need a handoff mechanism. Staging is overengineered; the answer is better task decomposition.
120	
121	## Procedure
122	
123	### Phase 1 — Cost Curve Analysis (H1)
124	
125	Can be done with existing session data. No new commissions needed if we have transcripts from prior runs.
126	
127	1. **Gather session transcripts** from commissioned sessions (post-commit `9d5bd96` for proper transcript capture).
128	2. **Build or use a cost analysis tool.** For each session:
129	   - Extract per-turn token usage (input, cache-read, cache-write, output)
130	   - Compute per-turn cost using Claude API pricing
131	   - Plot cost-per-turn over session lifetime
132	   - Calculate context tax percentage
133	3. **Evaluate H1.** Is the marginal cost increase real and significant?
134	
135	### Phase 2 — Quality and Staging Comparison (H2, H3)
136	
137	Run regardless of H1 outcome — quality is an independent question.
138	
139	4. **Select 2-3 commissions** large enough to stress a single session. Candidates:
140	   - Multi-file feature addition (new module + tests + integration)
141	   - Refactoring task touching many files
142	   - Bug fix requiring deep codebase understanding
143	
144	5. **Run each as a single long session.** Capture metrics and quality assessment.
145	
146	6. **Run each again, manually staged:**
147	   - Start normally
148	   - After ~40-50% of the work, halt
149	   - Write stage notes (what's done, what's next, key decisions)
150	   - Start a fresh session with the original spec + stage notes
151	   - Let it complete
152	
153	7. **Compare** quality and cost between variants.
154	
155	8. **Track merge outcomes** across all commissions for H3.
156	
157	### Phase 3 — Synthesis (H4)
158	
159	9. **Combine** cost data (Phase 1), quality data (Phase 2), and systemic data (Phase 2/H3).
160	10. **Find the crossover** — if one exists.
161	11. **Decide:** build staged commissions, invest in better task decomposition, or both.
162	
163	## Data Collection
164	
165	### Artifacts
166	
167	All artifacts go to `experiments/X010-staged-sessions/artifacts/`:
168	
169	- `staged-commissions-spec.md` — draft implementation spec for the staging mechanism (pre-existing)
170	- `cost-analysis.md` — per-session cost breakdowns and context tax calculations (Phase 1)
171	- `commission-{N}-single.md` — observations and metrics for each single-session run
172	- `commission-{N}-staged.md` — observations and metrics for each staged run
173	- `comparison.md` — side-by-side analysis
174	- `findings.md` — conclusions and decision
175	
176	### Session Data
177	
178	Session records and transcripts are captured automatically by the session funnel in `.nexus/sessions/`. Link to specific session IDs in observation notes.
179	
180	### Ethnographer Integration
181	
182	The ethnographer should capture Sean's experience reviewing single-session vs. staged output. Does the quality difference feel meaningful? Is the operational complexity worth it? This is relevant to X008 (Patron's Hands) — staging changes the patron's relationship with commissioned work.
183	
184	## Depends On
185	
186	- Session transcript capture working end-to-end (post-commit `9d5bd96`)
187	- At least a few commissioned session transcripts for Phase 1 cost analysis
188	- X007:H2 orientation analysis tool (for measuring re-orientation cost in staged sessions)
189	- For Phase 2: commissions worth running 2+ times
190	
191	## Risks
192	
193	- **Pricing changes:** Claude API pricing may change between sessions, invalidating cost comparisons. Pin to a specific pricing model in the analysis.
194	- **Caching variability:** Cache hit rates vary based on system prompt similarity, time between requests, and provider-side eviction. Two runs of the "same" session may have different caching profiles. Note cache hit rates in all measurements.
195	- **Commission selection bias:** Need commissions in the sweet spot — large enough to show the cost curve, small enough to run multiple times without burning budget.
196	- **Manual staging artifacts:** In Phase 2, Sean writes the stage notes. This tests the mechanism with high-quality handoff notes — an artificer writing its own notes may do better or worse. Known limitation; the experiment tests whether the mechanism *can* work, not whether agents are good at using it.
197	- **Small sample size:** Exploratory, not statistically rigorous. Strong effects will be visible; subtle ones won't.
198	- **Conflating staging with task decomposition:** The experiment may reveal that the real lever is smaller commissions, not staged sessions. That's a valid finding — it just means the answer is "write better specs" rather than "build staging infrastructure." The experiment should be open to this conclusion.
199

# X010 — Staged Sessions

## Research Question

What is the relationship between session length, cost, and output quality for autonomous commissions? Specifically: do long sessions cost more per unit of productive work? Do they produce worse output? And if either is true, does breaking work into shorter sessions capture the benefits without unacceptable handoff losses?

## Background

There are two independent reasons to suspect that shorter sessions might be better than longer ones:

**Cost.** LLM API pricing is token-based. Each turn in a session sends the full conversation history as input. As a session gets longer, every new turn pays for a larger input context — even with caching, cache-read tokens aren't free. This means the cost of each successive turn grows with the session's accumulated context. A 50-turn session doesn't cost 50× a 1-turn session; it costs significantly more, because turns 40-50 are each paying to re-read the context from turns 1-39.

**Quality.** LLM attention degrades over long contexts. Earlier decisions get "forgotten," the agent loses coherence with its own prior work, and output quality may decline — especially in long autonomous sessions with heavy tool use, where each turn adds tool calls and results to the context. Shorter, focused sessions might simply produce tighter work: fewer loose ends, less scope drift, more coherent commits.

These are independent claims. Cost might be a problem even if quality is fine. Quality might be a problem even if cost is acceptable. Both might be problems, or neither. The experiment needs to measure them separately.

Beyond per-session economics, there are **systemic costs** to long-running commissions. A commission that holds a worktree for 30 minutes while other commissions are running has a longer window for `main` to diverge, increasing the probability of a failed fast-forward merge. Shorter sessions (or smaller commissions) reduce this conflict window. Failed merges are expensive — the commission's entire output is discarded, the tokens are wasted, and the work must be re-done or manually recovered. The expected cost of merge failures should factor into the economic analysis.

This connects to X007:H2 (Orientation Cost Dominates), which measures how much of a session is spent orienting. X010 asks the upstream question: is the cost/quality profile of long sessions bad enough that paying the re-orientation tax multiple times is still a net win?

### Relationship to X004

X004 (Iteration Context) asks a closely related question: when an agent iterates on its own prior work, how much context about prior sessions should it get? Its three variants — no context, summary context, full context (via session resume/fork) — directly parallel the handoff problem in staged sessions. X010's "stage notes" are essentially X004's "summary context" variant.

Key differences:

- **X004 is about iteration on completed work** (amendments to a delivered commission). X010 is about continuation of in-progress work within a single commission.
- **X004 includes a "full context" variant** (resume the prior session with full history). X010's premise is that fresh sessions are better — it doesn't test resumption.
- **X004 asks whether context can hurt** (over-anchoring on prior approach). X010 assumes the summary helps and asks whether it's *sufficient*.

Findings from either experiment inform the other. If X004 finds that summary context outperforms full context, that validates X010's stage-notes approach. If X010 finds that staged sessions lose too much context, that suggests X004's "full context" variant may be necessary for iteration too. Data collection instruments (orientation cost analysis, quality assessment) are shared.

## Hypotheses

### H1 — Long Sessions Have Increasing Marginal Cost

The cost per productive turn increases as a session gets longer. Specifically: the last 10 turns of a long session (30+ turns) cost meaningfully more than the first 10 turns, even though the productive work per turn is comparable. The dominant factor is accumulated input context — each turn pays to re-read the entire conversation history.

This is partly true by construction (input context grows, so input tokens per turn grow), but the question is *how much it matters* in practice. With aggressive caching, the marginal cost increase might be trivial. Or it might be substantial — that's what we need to measure.

**Measurement:**
- Analyze session transcripts from commissioned sessions (existing or new)
- For each turn, extract: input tokens (fresh + cache-read + cache-write), output tokens, estimated cost
- Plot cost-per-turn over the session lifetime
- Calculate: what percentage of total session cost is "context tax" (input tokens that are re-reads of prior conversation, not new information)?

**Thresholds:**
- **Confirmed:** The last quarter of turns costs >2× per turn compared to the first quarter, consistently across sessions. Context tax accounts for >30% of total session cost.
- **Partially confirmed:** Marginal cost increases but modestly (1.3-2× ratio). Context tax is 15-30%. Optimization is a nice-to-have.
- **Refuted:** Marginal cost increase is <1.3× (caching absorbs most of it). Context tax is <15%. Long sessions are cost-efficient enough — no cost-motivated reason to stage.

### H2 — Shorter Sessions Produce Better Output

Independent of cost, shorter focused sessions produce higher-quality work than long sessions tackling the same total scope. Quality here means: fewer defects, less scope drift, tighter task adherence, more coherent code.

The mechanism would be cognitive focus — a fresh context window lets the agent bring full attention to a well-scoped chunk of work, rather than juggling an increasingly large mental model. This is analogous to the human experience of "fresh eyes" after a break.

**Measurement:**
- Run the same commission both ways: single long session vs. manually staged (2-3 shorter sessions with handoff notes)
- Compare output quality:
  - Defect count (bugs, broken tests, incomplete implementations)
  - Scope drift (changes outside the commission scope)
  - Code coherence (does the code contradict itself? do later changes undo or clash with earlier ones?)
  - Task adherence (did the output match the spec?)
  - Commit quality (message clarity, atomic commits vs. sprawling ones — a proxy for agent coherence)
- Also compare within single long sessions: is the quality of work in the first half measurably better than the second half?

**Thresholds:**
- **Confirmed:** Staged output has fewer defects and less drift than single-session output, consistently. And/or: late-session work within a single session is measurably worse than early-session work.
- **Partially confirmed:** Quality difference is visible but small. Some commission types show it more than others.
- **Refuted:** No measurable quality difference. Long sessions maintain coherence fine. The "context degradation" concern is overblown for sessions at our current scale.

### H3 — Systemic Costs Compound the Case

Beyond per-session economics, long-running commissions carry systemic costs that shorter sessions avoid:

- **Merge conflict probability:** A commission that runs for 30 minutes has a larger window for concurrent commissions to merge to `main` first, causing a fast-forward failure. The cost of a failed merge is high — all tokens spent on the commission are wasted, and the work must be re-done or manually recovered.
- **Time-to-feedback:** A long session delays the moment the patron can review the work. Shorter sessions (especially if each stage merges independently) provide faster feedback loops.
- **Blast radius:** If a long session goes off the rails at turn 40, turns 1-39 of useful work may be lost. Staged sessions that commit and (potentially) merge incrementally contain the blast radius.

Note: the current staged commissions design (see [`artifacts/staged-commissions-spec.md`](artifacts/staged-commissions-spec.md)) keeps all stages on one branch — the merge only happens at the end. This means staging *does not* reduce merge conflict probability in its current form. A variant design where each stage merges to `main` before the next stage starts *would* reduce conflict probability, but requires each stage to be independently mergeable. This is a design question the experiment should inform.

**Note:** The staged commissions implementation spec is still a draft with open design questions — particularly around the stage notes file path, loop guards, crash behavior, prompt structure for continuation sessions, and whether stages should merge independently. This experiment should inform those decisions.

**Measurement:**
- Track merge outcomes (success/fail) across all commissions, correlated with session duration
- For failed merges: what was the wall-clock time of the commission? How far had `main` moved?
- Estimate the expected cost of merge failures: `P(failure) × cost_of_session`
- Compare: would shorter sessions have avoided the conflict?

**Thresholds:**
- **Confirmed:** Merge failure rate correlates with session duration. Expected cost of failures is significant relative to session cost.
- **Refuted:** Merge failures are rare regardless of session length (because concurrent commissions in the same workshop are rare, or because work doesn't overlap).

### H4 — Net Economics Favor Staging (Above a Threshold)

Combining H1-H3: for commissions above some size threshold, the total cost of multiple staged sessions (including re-orientation tax per stage) is lower than a single long session, with equal or better quality. Below that threshold, single sessions are fine.

This is the synthesis hypothesis — it depends on the findings from H1, H2, and H3. The goal is to find the crossover point, if one exists.

**Measurement:**
- For each commission pair (single vs. staged), compute:
  - Total cost (USD) for each variant
  - Cost per useful commit as an efficiency proxy
  - Re-orientation cost per stage (via X007:H2 analysis tool)
  - Quality-adjusted cost (if staged quality differs, factor that in)
  - Expected merge failure cost (from H3 data)
- Look for the crossover: at what single-session length does staging become net-positive?

**Possible outcomes:**
- **Clear crossover exists:** Staging is better above N turns (or $N). Build the mechanism, set guidance for commission sizing.
- **Staging always wins:** Multiple short sessions are better at every scale. Make it the default, and teach the sage to decompose work accordingly.
- **Staging never wins:** Re-orientation cost and handoff losses eat any savings. Single sessions are fine. Invest in better commission specs instead.
- **Depends on commission type:** Feature additions hand off cleanly; deep refactors don't. The mechanism is useful but not universal — needs guidance on when to use it.
- **Just write smaller commissions:** The real lesson is that the patron (or sage) should decompose work into smaller independent commissions rather than using staging. Each commission is self-contained, merges independently, and doesn't need a handoff mechanism. Staging is overengineered; the answer is better task decomposition.

## Procedure

### Phase 1 — Cost Curve Analysis (H1)

Can be done with existing session data. No new commissions needed if we have transcripts from prior runs.

1. **Gather session transcripts** from commissioned sessions (post-commit `9d5bd96` for proper transcript capture).
2. **Build or use a cost analysis tool.** For each session:
   - Extract per-turn token usage (input, cache-read, cache-write, output)
   - Compute per-turn cost using Claude API pricing
   - Plot cost-per-turn over session lifetime
   - Calculate context tax percentage
3. **Evaluate H1.** Is the marginal cost increase real and significant?

### Phase 2 — Quality and Staging Comparison (H2, H3)

Run regardless of H1 outcome — quality is an independent question.

4. **Select 2-3 commissions** large enough to stress a single session. Candidates:
   - Multi-file feature addition (new module + tests + integration)
   - Refactoring task touching many files
   - Bug fix requiring deep codebase understanding

5. **Run each as a single long session.** Capture metrics and quality assessment.

6. **Run each again, manually staged:**
   - Start normally
   - After ~40-50% of the work, halt
   - Write stage notes (what's done, what's next, key decisions)
   - Start a fresh session with the original spec + stage notes
   - Let it complete

7. **Compare** quality and cost between variants.

8. **Track merge outcomes** across all commissions for H3.

### Phase 3 — Synthesis (H4)

9. **Combine** cost data (Phase 1), quality data (Phase 2), and systemic data (Phase 2/H3).
10. **Find the crossover** — if one exists.
11. **Decide:** build staged commissions, invest in better task decomposition, or both.

## Data Collection

### Artifacts

All artifacts go to `experiments/X010-staged-sessions/artifacts/`:

- `staged-commissions-spec.md` — draft implementation spec for the staging mechanism (pre-existing)
- `cost-analysis.md` — per-session cost breakdowns and context tax calculations (Phase 1)
- `commission-{N}-single.md` — observations and metrics for each single-session run
- `commission-{N}-staged.md` — observations and metrics for each staged run
- `comparison.md` — side-by-side analysis
- `findings.md` — conclusions and decision

### Session Data

Session records and transcripts are captured automatically by the session funnel in `.nexus/sessions/`. Link to specific session IDs in observation notes.

### Ethnographer Integration

The ethnographer should capture Sean's experience reviewing single-session vs. staged output. Does the quality difference feel meaningful? Is the operational complexity worth it? This is relevant to X008 (Patron's Hands) — staging changes the patron's relationship with commissioned work.

## Depends On

- Session transcript capture working end-to-end (post-commit `9d5bd96`)
- At least a few commissioned session transcripts for Phase 1 cost analysis
- X007:H2 orientation analysis tool (for measuring re-orientation cost in staged sessions)
- For Phase 2: commissions worth running 2+ times

## Risks

- **Pricing changes:** Claude API pricing may change between sessions, invalidating cost comparisons. Pin to a specific pricing model in the analysis.
- **Caching variability:** Cache hit rates vary based on system prompt similarity, time between requests, and provider-side eviction. Two runs of the "same" session may have different caching profiles. Note cache hit rates in all measurements.
- **Commission selection bias:** Need commissions in the sweet spot — large enough to show the cost curve, small enough to run multiple times without burning budget.
- **Manual staging artifacts:** In Phase 2, Sean writes the stage notes. This tests the mechanism with high-quality handoff notes — an artificer writing its own notes may do better or worse. Known limitation; the experiment tests whether the mechanism *can* work, not whether agents are good at using it.
- **Small sample size:** Exploratory, not statistically rigorous. Strong effects will be visible; subtle ones won't.
- **Conflating staging with task decomposition:** The experiment may reveal that the real lever is smaller commissions, not staged sessions. That's a valid finding — it just means the answer is "write better specs" rather than "build staging infrastructure." The experiment should be open to this conclusion.

5	# X011 — Context Debt
6	
7	## Research Question
8	
9	How much of an agent's context window is consumed by tool output it will never reference again, and can we reduce this without hurting agent effectiveness?
10	
11	## Origin
12	
13	Observed during X007 (First Contact): the artificer's context jumped from ~30K to ~36K tokens at `npm install` — output that was never referenced again but remained in the context window for the rest of the session, re-read (and paid for) on every subsequent turn.
14	
15	The agent partially mitigated this itself (`npm install 2>&1 | tail -10`) but the output still entered the conversation. Every `ls`, `git log`, test run, and build output accumulates the same way — context debt that the agent pays interest on for the remainder of the session.
16	
17	## Hypothesis
18	
19	A significant fraction of context window usage (and therefore cost) comes from tool output that is consumed once and never referenced again. Providing agents with output-controlled execution tools — commands that suppress output on success, truncate to N lines, or summarize results — would reduce context growth without reducing agent effectiveness.
20	
21	## Possible Approaches
22	
23	- **A `run` or `exec` tool** that returns exit code + summary on success, last N lines on failure. Full output never enters context. Agent chooses when to use quiet mode vs. verbose Bash.
24	- **Instruction-level guidance** teaching agents to redirect verbose output to files and read selectively.
25	- **Framework-level truncation** in the session provider — auto-truncate tool results above a threshold.
26	- **Output policies** — per-command rules (suppress npm install on success, always show test failures, truncate build output to errors only).
27	
28	## Open Questions
29	
30	- How much context is actually "dead" output by the end of a typical session? Need to measure this across multiple sessions.
31	- Does truncating output hurt agent effectiveness? Some agents use earlier output for reference (e.g., re-reading an `ls` result to remember file structure).
32	- Is this better solved by the agent (instructions to use `tail`/redirect), the tools (quiet-mode execution), or the framework (auto-truncation)?
33	- How does this interact with X010 (Staged Sessions)? If context debt drives sessions to fill up faster, reducing it extends the useful life of a single session.
34	
35	## Depends On
36	
37	- Session transcript data from multiple commissions (for measuring dead output)
38	- X007/X010 cost analysis tooling (for quantifying the cost impact)

# X011 — Context Debt

## Research Question

How much of an agent's context window is consumed by tool output it will never reference again, and can we reduce this without hurting agent effectiveness?

## Origin

Observed during X007 (First Contact): the artificer's context jumped from ~30K to ~36K tokens at `npm install` — output that was never referenced again but remained in the context window for the rest of the session, re-read (and paid for) on every subsequent turn.

The agent partially mitigated this itself (`npm install 2>&1 | tail -10`) but the output still entered the conversation. Every `ls`, `git log`, test run, and build output accumulates the same way — context debt that the agent pays interest on for the remainder of the session.

## Hypothesis

A significant fraction of context window usage (and therefore cost) comes from tool output that is consumed once and never referenced again. Providing agents with output-controlled execution tools — commands that suppress output on success, truncate to N lines, or summarize results — would reduce context growth without reducing agent effectiveness.

## Possible Approaches

- **A `run` or `exec` tool** that returns exit code + summary on success, last N lines on failure. Full output never enters context. Agent chooses when to use quiet mode vs. verbose Bash.
- **Instruction-level guidance** teaching agents to redirect verbose output to files and read selectively.
- **Framework-level truncation** in the session provider — auto-truncate tool results above a threshold.
- **Output policies** — per-command rules (suppress npm install on success, always show test failures, truncate build output to errors only).

## Open Questions

- How much context is actually "dead" output by the end of a typical session? Need to measure this across multiple sessions.
- Does truncating output hurt agent effectiveness? Some agents use earlier output for reference (e.g., re-reading an `ls` result to remember file structure).
- Is this better solved by the agent (instructions to use `tail`/redirect), the tools (quiet-mode execution), or the framework (auto-truncation)?
- How does this interact with X010 (Staged Sessions)? If context debt drives sessions to fill up faster, reducing it extends the useful life of a single session.

## Depends On

- Session transcript data from multiple commissions (for measuring dead output)
- X007/X010 cost analysis tooling (for quantifying the cost impact)

3	---
4	
5	# X013 — Commission Outcomes
6	
7	## Research Question
8	
9	What predicts good outcomes from autonomous commissions — and can
10	tracking outcomes over time serve as a health indicator for the system?
11	
12	## Background
13	
14	The system has been running autonomous commissions since 2026-03-25.
15	Failures occur, sometimes critically. But the attribution is unclear:
16	when a commission produces a wrong result, is that a spec quality
17	problem (patron's craft), a complexity problem (system capability
18	limit), or an agent deficiency (model or curriculum)? These three
19	causes have different fixes. Without separating them, every failure
20	is ambiguous and every improvement effort is a guess.
21	
22	This experiment creates the tracking infrastructure to answer that
23	question over time — and tests whether revision rate (the fraction
24	of commissions requiring follow-up work) functions as a meaningful
25	health indicator for the system as it matures.
26	
27	## Hypotheses
28	
29	### H1 — Spec Quality Predicts Output Quality
30	
31	Commission spec quality is a significant predictor of output quality,
32	independent of task complexity. A well-written spec — clear scope
33	boundaries, explicit event flows, unambiguous acceptance criteria —
34	produces better outcomes regardless of task complexity.
35	
36	Output quality is measured on two axes:
37	
38	- **Requirement satisfaction** (`outcome`) — did the commission meet
39	  its requirements? Measured as success / partial / wrong / abandoned.
40	- **Code quality** (`code_quality_agent`) — is the code well-written
41	  independent of requirements? Measured by the autonomous quality
42	  scorer (see Data Collection — Quality Scorer). This is a composite
43	  score (1.0–3.0) averaged across multiple independent reviewer runs,
44	  covering test quality, code structure, error handling, and codebase
45	  consistency.
46	
47	Spec quality is defined against objective, agent-independent criteria
48	(see Data Collection). The same spec may produce different results
49	with different agents; that variation is agent deficiency, not spec
50	quality variation.
51	
52	**If true:** Commission writing is a learnable craft that meaningfully
53	improves outcomes. Two levers exist: patron skill development (better
54	spec-writing practice, templates, checklists) and system-level
55	correction (agentic preparation — e.g., Sage consultation — that
56	identifies and repairs deficient specs before dispatch). These are
57	complementary: patron skill sets the baseline; agentic correction
58	catches what remains.
59	
60	**If false:** Spec quality is a weak predictor. The bottleneck is
61	elsewhere — complexity handling, model capability, or curriculum quality.
62	
63	*Note: H1 is only testable if spec quality is rated before outcome is
64	known. See Data Collection — contamination of pre-dispatch ratings is
65	the primary methodological risk.*
66	
67	### H2 — Complexity Threshold
68	
69	There is a complexity threshold above which failure rate increases
70	significantly. Below the threshold, task complexity has little effect
71	on outcome. Above it, failures cluster. The threshold is not fixed —
72	it may shift as curricula improve, models improve, or oversight
73	mechanisms are added.
74	
75	**If true:** Complexity tier is a useful dispatch signal. Tasks above
76	the threshold need different handling — more oversight, staged
77	delivery, or a sage consultation step — rather than just better specs.
78	
79	**If false:** Complexity is a weak predictor once spec quality is
80	controlled for. Good specs can bridge complex tasks without special
81	handling.
82	
83	*Note: Complexity ratings carry the same contamination risk as spec
84	quality ratings. Both must be recorded at dispatch time, before outcome
85	is known. See Data Collection.*
86	
87	### H3 — Revision Rate as System Health Indicator
88	
89	Revision rate (fraction of commissions requiring follow-up fixes)
90	declines as the system matures. A declining rate indicates some
91	combination of: improved spec-writing practice, improved curricula,
92	better anima capability, or better complexity-matching. A flat or
93	rising rate indicates something isn't improving despite investment.
94	
95	The early period of this experiment establishes the descriptive
96	baseline. There is no external reference class to target; the primary
97	signal is change over time within the system. External comparison to
98	industry rework rates is a future analysis goal — see Future Work.
99	
100	**If true:** Revision rate is a leading indicator worth tracking
101	continuously — an early warning signal before failures become critical.
102	
103	**If false:** Revision rate is noisy and doesn't track system quality
104	meaningfully. Use other signals.
105	
106	### H4 — Attribution Becomes Possible
107	
108	By controlling for spec quality (H1) and complexity (H2), failures
109	can be attributed to root cause. The taxonomy has four causes:
110	
111	- **Patron craft** — spec quality was low; the failure traces to how
112	  the commission was written
113	- **System limits** — complexity was above threshold; the task was
114	  beyond current handling regardless of spec quality
115	- **Agent deficiency** — spec was adequate, complexity was appropriate,
116	  agent still failed; the failure traces to model or curriculum
117	- **Patron requirement error** — spec was clear and well-formed, but
118	  described the wrong thing; the agent did exactly what was asked and
119	  the result was still wrong
120	
121	This attribution is currently impossible because the contributing
122	factors are unmeasured.
123	
124	**If true:** We can direct improvement effort rather than treating
125	every failure as undifferentiated: better spec-writing practice,
126	higher-complexity curriculum, model or oversight changes, or
127	improved requirement validation before dispatch.
128	
129	*Note: H4 is the most aspirational of the four hypotheses. It requires
130	H1 and H2 to hold cleanly, the contamination problem to be solved, and
131	sufficient N to detect signal. Treat it as the north star for analysis,
132	not an expected early result. Even with objective spec quality scoring,
133	different agents may produce different results on the same spec —
134	the goal is to hold agent constant when comparing spec quality effects,
135	or hold spec constant when comparing agents.*
136	
137	## Data Collection
138	
139	### Data Architecture
140	
141	Commission data is split across two tiers, optimized for different
142	access patterns:
143	
144	1. **Commission log** (`experiments/data/commission-log.yaml`) — a lean,
145	   human-navigable YAML file containing patron-subjective judgments and
146	   dispatch metadata. This is the primary instrument for browsing,
147	   annotating, and discussing commissions interactively.
148	
149	2. **Per-commission artifacts** (`experiments/data/commissions/<id>/`) —
150	   the full evidentiary record: session telemetry, quality scorer output,
151	   commission body, dispatch log, review notes, and scoring context.
152	   These are the source of truth for objective/automated data.
153	
154	The unified analytical dataset is assembled on demand by joining the
155	commission log with per-commission artifacts. The log's `id` field is
156	the join key to the per-commission folder name. See the
157	[commissions README](../data/commissions/README.md) for the artifact
158	schema and join documentation.
159	
160	This separation is deliberate: the commission log stays small enough
161	for a human or agent to read end-to-end, while the full data richness
162	is available for analysis without cluttering the navigable instrument.
163	
164	### Commission Log (Standing Research Instrument)
165	
166	The commission log lives at `experiments/data/commission-log.yaml`.
167	It is not experiment-specific — it is a standing research instrument
168	that will accumulate data across multiple experiments over time. X013
169	defines the first analytical framework applied to it; future experiments
170	may draw on the same corpus.
171	
172	Annotation is split into two moments. Pre-dispatch ratings — complexity
173	and spec quality — must be recorded before the commission runs and the
174	outcome is known. Conflating pre- and post-dispatch ratings in a single
175	review step is the primary methodological risk: failed commissions will
176	feel like they had weaker specs and higher complexity in retrospect,
177	producing artificial correlation.
178	
179	#### At Dispatch
180	
181	Fields marked `auto` are populated by `inscribe.sh` when a commission
182	is posted. Fields marked `patron` must be filled in by Sean at dispatch
183	time — this is the contamination-safe window.
184	
185	| Field | Source | Values | Notes |
186	|---|---|---|---|
187	| `id` | auto | string | writ ID from Clerk |
188	| `title` | auto | string | extracted from commission body |
189	| `codex` | auto | string | target codex name |
190	| `complexity` | patron | 1 / 2 / 3 / 5 / 8 / 13 / 21 | **rate now, before outcome is known** |
191	| `spec_quality_pre` | patron | strong / adequate / weak | **rate now, before outcome is known** |
192	
193	**Complexity (Fibonacci scale — patron self-assessment):**
194	
195	Use Scrum story point intuition. Rough anchors:
196	- *1–2* — additive, self-contained, single area of the system, no integration work
197	- *3–5* — multiple touch points, moderate cross-system interaction
198	- *8–13* — core lifecycle, dispatch logic, event chains, or broad behavioral changes
199	- *21* — system-wide; touches core abstractions with broad downstream effects
200	
201	**Spec quality criteria (at dispatch — mountain-spec adequacy):**
202	
203	Spec quality is assessed at the requirements/product-owner level.
204	It does not reward implementation detail — that is the anima's
205	domain. A spec scoreable by someone who knows the product goal but
206	nothing about the codebase is a well-formed spec.
207	
208	- *Strong* — success is unambiguous (you'd recognize it when you saw
209	  it), scope boundary is clear, motivation/context is present,
210	  non-obvious product-level cases are addressed
211	- *Adequate* — success is recognizable, scope mostly clear, major
212	  cases covered
213	- *Weak* — unclear what success looks like, ambiguous scope, or
214	  missing key context
215	
216	#### At Outcome Review
217	
218	All fields are filled in by Sean at review time.
219	
220	| Field | Source | Values | Notes |
221	|---|---|---|---|
222	| `outcome` | patron | success / partial / wrong / abandoned | patron assessment of the commission result |
223	| `revision_required` | patron | boolean | whether follow-up work was needed |
224	| `spec_quality_post` | patron | strong / adequate / weak | retrospective — record separately from `spec_quality_pre`; divergence is data |
225	| `reviewed_at` | coco | ISO date / null | date patron reviewed this commission with Coco; null = not reviewed |
226	| `failure_mode` | patron | spec_ambiguous / requirement_wrong / execution_error / complexity_overrun / broken / incomplete | optional; best guess at root cause |
227	| `reviewed_by_ethnographer` | ethnographer | boolean | mark after ethnographer has probed this case |
228	| `note` | patron | string | optional; meta info about the record itself |
229	
230	**Outcome criteria:**
231	- *Success* — did what was asked, shippable with minimal or no fixes
232	- *Partial* — did most of it, needed meaningful follow-up work
233	- *Wrong* — completed but missed the point; required rework or redo
234	- *Abandoned* — never executed, got stuck, or was cancelled
235	
236	### Per-Commission Artifacts (Objective Data)
237	
238	Objective and automated data lives in per-commission artifact folders
239	rather than the commission log. This keeps the log lean while
240	preserving full data richness for analysis. Key artifacts:
241	
242	| Artifact | Source | Content |
243	|---|---|---|
244	| `sessions/*.yaml` | The Laboratory (auto) | Session telemetry: cost, duration, token usage, timing |
245	| `quality-blind.yaml` | Quality scorer (auto) | Code quality scores without spec context |
246	| `quality-aware.yaml` | Quality scorer (auto) | Code quality scores with spec and requirement coverage |
247	| `commission.md` | `inscribe.sh` (auto) | The writ body as dispatched |
248	| `dispatch.log` | `inscribe.sh` (auto) | Timestamped dispatch lifecycle log |
249	| `review.md` | Patron (manual) | Patron review notes and scorer summary |
250	| `quality-context/` | Quality scorer (auto) | Diff, changed files, context — makes scoring reproducible |
251	
252	The quality scorer runs post-commission as part of `inscribe.sh`
253	(Phase 5). It is independent of patron assessment and not subject to
254	timing contamination. See the Quality Scorer section below.
255	
256	Session telemetry provides `cost_usd`, `duration_ms`, and token
257	breakdowns. These are available for analysis via the per-commission
258	artifacts without needing to be duplicated in the log.
259	
260	The patron's manual dispatch-time annotation (`complexity`,
261	`spec_quality_pre`) remains the primary experimental input — it
262	captures Sean's subjective assessment at the moment of dispatch, which
263	is itself a data point. Automated data supplements and validates it;
264	it does not replace it.
265	
266	### Future Automation
267	
268	**Spec scorer anima** — reads commission spec text and produces
269	structured quality and complexity scores using the same criteria and
270	Fibonacci scale as patron self-assessment. Independent of timing
271	contamination; backfillable. Output would land in per-commission
272	artifacts alongside quality scorer output. See Depends On — Future.
273	
274	### Ethnographer Access and Interview Practice
275	
276	The ethnographer reads the commission log at the start of each session.
277	Filter for recently completed commissions not yet marked
278	`reviewed_by_ethnographer`. Prioritize by outcome (wrong, partial, and
279	abandoned before success) and complexity (higher Fibonacci values
280	first). Use filtered cases to select specific commissions to probe —
281	not every commission warrants a dedicated interview probe.
282	
283	This replaces relying on session notes as the primary source of what
284	happened. Session notes capture what Coco observed; the commission log
285	captures Sean's own assessment. Both are useful; they are different
286	data.
287	
288	This also represents a shift in the ethnographer's standing operating
289	mode: commission-specific probing should be consistent and
290	agenda-driven, not incidental. Update ethnographer standing
291	instructions accordingly.
292	
293	When a commission is selected for probing, the interview questions are:
294	
295	- Walk me through how you evaluated that result. What were you looking for?
296	- Was there a moment where you could tell what went wrong (or right)?
297	- What would have made the spec stronger?
298	- Would you have dispatched this differently knowing what you know now?
299	
300	### Quality Scorer (Autonomous Code Review)
301	
302	The quality scorer is an autonomous agent that reviews commission
303	output against a fixed rubric. It provides the `code_quality_agent`
304	signal — an independent, reproducible measure of code quality that
305	is not subject to the patron's retrospective bias.
306	
307	**Why this exists:** The `outcome` field measures requirement
308	satisfaction (did the commission do what was asked?), not code quality
309	(is the code well-written?). Two commissions can both succeed while
310	producing wildly different code. Without a quality signal beyond
311	pass/fail, H1's claim about "output quality" reduces to a weaker
312	claim about requirement fulfillment. The quality scorer closes this
313	gap.
314	
315	**Instrument design:** See
316	`experiments/instruments/anima-quality-scorer/proposal.md` for full
317	motivation and design rationale (archival — the original instrument).
318	Key properties:
319	
320	- **Four dimensions, 3-point scale:** test quality, code structure,
321	  error handling, codebase consistency. Composite score 1.0–3.0.
322	- **Multi-run averaging:** 3 independent runs per review (increase
323	  to 5 if inter-run SD > 0.5). Treats the LLM as a noisy sensor.
324	- **Spec-blind mode** (experimental instrument): reviewer sees only
325	  the code, not the commission spec. Produces a quality score
326	  orthogonal to requirement satisfaction.
327	- **Spec-aware mode** (operational acceptance tool): reviewer also
328	  sees the spec and scores a fifth dimension — requirement coverage.
329	  Useful for commission acceptance independent of the experiment.
330	- **Versioned prompt:** The complete prompt (system prompt + rubric +
331	  user template) is versioned as a unit. Each review artifact records
332	  which version produced it. Prompt changes require a new version.
333	- **Isolated execution:** Reviewer runs with all tools disabled, in
334	  an empty directory, with no access to project configuration. All
335	  context is in the prompt. Pure text-in/text-out instrument.
336	- **Backfillable:** Code quality is a property of the artifact, not
337	  the moment. Existing commissions can be scored retroactively.
338	
339	**Operational tooling:**
340	
341	- `bin/quality-review.sh` — run a single-mode review (blind or aware)
342	- `bin/quality-review-full.sh` — run both modes in parallel
343	- Artifacts land in per-commission folders: `quality-blind.yaml`,
344	  `quality-aware.yaml`, and `quality-context/`
345	
346	### Revision Rate Tracking
347	
348	Long-term: revision rate is computed from the writ graph — the
349	percentage of patron commissions with at least one inbound `revises`
350	relationship. This is automated once the writ relationships commission
351	ships and enables richer queries: fix-of-fix chains, what fraction of
352	total commission volume is purely remediation work, etc.
353	
354	Interim: `revision_required` in the commission log is a manual boolean
355	that approximates the same signal until writ relationships are in place.
356	
357	Track revision rate over time and plot alongside system milestones
358	(curriculum updates, model changes, new animas) to correlate
359	improvements with rate changes.
360	
361	**On noise:** A rolling window — whether count-based or time-based —
362	has noise proportional to window size. At current commission volumes,
363	any rolling window will be small enough to produce misleading swings.
364	This is unavoidable early. Track the rate, note when the early period's
365	rate serves as the descriptive baseline, and avoid strong conclusions
366	before N ≥ 20. The trend over time is the signal; individual data
367	points are not.
368	
369	## Depends On
370	
371	### Required (experiment cannot start without these)
372	
373	- **Commission log file** — `experiments/data/commission-log.yaml`,
374	  a standing instrument. Coco scans for unfilled dispatch-time entries
375	  and prompts Sean to fill them at session start.
376	- **Dispatch pipeline** — `inscribe.sh` orchestrates the full cycle:
377	  post → dispatch → capture session record → scaffold log entry →
378	  quality scoring. Auto-populates per-commission artifacts.
379	- Minimum ~10 commissions for initial pattern analysis; ~30 for
380	  meaningful regression.
381	
382	### Operational (built, running)
383	
384	- **Quality scorer** — autonomous code quality reviewer. Instrument
385	  definitions at `experiments/instruments/spec-blind-quality-scorer/`
386	  and `experiments/instruments/spec-aware-quality-scorer/`. Runner
387	  package at `packages/instruments/`. Output lands in per-commission
388	  artifacts. Strengthens H1 by providing a code quality signal
389	  independent of requirement satisfaction. See
390	  Data Collection — Quality Scorer.
391	- **Session telemetry** — The Laboratory auto-generates session YAML
392	  with cost, duration, and token usage. Lands in per-commission
393	  `sessions/` directory.
394	
395	### Future (would strengthen the experiment; not required)
396	
397	- **Spec scorer anima** — reads commission spec text and produces
398	  structured quality and complexity scores using the same criteria and
399	  Fibonacci scale as patron self-assessment. Independent of timing
400	  contamination; backfillable. Output would land in per-commission
401	  artifacts. See Future Automation in Data Collection.
402	- **Writ relationships** — typed directed relationships between writs
403	  (`revises`, `blocks`, `depends-on`). Would enable `revision_required`
404	  to be computed from the writ graph rather than a manual boolean, and
405	  richer revision rate analysis (fix-of-fix chains, remediation volume).
406	- **Patron assessment tool** — `assess-writ` CLI command to record
407	  outcome on the writ record directly, reducing manual log editing.
408	
409	## Risks
410	
411	- **Outcome-contaminated ratings.** Both `complexity` and
412	  `spec_quality_pre` must be recorded at dispatch time. If either is
413	  rated after outcome is known, the scores will correlate artificially
414	  with outcome — which is the thing we're trying to measure. The
415	  pre-dispatch fields in the commission log are the structural
416	  mitigation; behavioral discipline alone is not reliable.
417	
418	- **Small N early.** The first 10–15 commissions will be too noisy for
419	  pattern analysis. Don't draw conclusions until 20+.
420	
421	- **Retrospective bias.** `spec_quality_post`, `outcome`, and
422	  `failure_mode` are all retrospective. This is unavoidable. The
423	  pre-dispatch annotation mitigates it for spec quality and complexity;
424	  the remaining fields should be read as qualitative signal, not
425	  objective measurement.
426	
427	- **H4 attribution limits.** Even with objective spec quality scoring,
428	  different agents produce different results on the same spec —
429	  `failure_mode: execution_error` may actually reflect a harder spec
430	  than the ratings captured. Attribution will be probabilistic, not
431	  certain. H4 is the analytical north star; treat early attributions
432	  as hypotheses to revisit as N grows.
433	
434	- **Scope creep.** This experiment generates data that could answer
435	  many questions. Keep analysis focused on the four hypotheses. Resist
436	  adding new data collection without a hypothesis to test.
437	
438	## Future Work
439	
440	- **External baseline for revision rate.** Software engineering
441	  literature on rework rates — agile sprint defect rates, code review
442	  rejection rates, bug-to-feature ratios — could provide rough external
443	  comparison for H3 once internal data accumulates. Not required for the
444	  experiment, but useful context for published findings. Identify
445	  relevant data sources and assess applicability.
446	
447	- **Spec scorer calibration.** Once the spec scorer anima is running
448	  and empirical outcome data exists, test whether anima-rated spec
449	  quality predicts outcomes as well as or better than patron
450	  self-assessment. If the signals align, consider using the anima score
451	  as a dispatch gate — flagging or holding weak specs before they run.
452	
453	- **Writ graph analysis.** Once writ relationships are in place,
454	  revision rate expands into richer analysis: fix-of-fix chains,
455	  time-to-revision, whether certain animas or complexity tiers
456	  concentrate revision work, etc.
457	
458	- **Technical spec quality (X014).** A related but distinct question:
459	  do technically detailed specs outperform mountain-quality specs on
460	  comparable tasks? This is a direct empirical test of the mountain-
461	  spec philosophy and requires a designed intervention — deliberately
462	  varying implementation detail level across comparable commissions —
463	  not passive observation. Technical detail ratings can be backfilled
464	  from spec text retroactively (they are not outcome-contaminated),
465	  so existing X013 entries can seed the X014 corpus. See X014 draft.
